drafts.questionText: When a stack is implemented with an array and a single index pointing to the top element, what is the time complexity of push and pop?
options: O(1) because the index moves by one without shifting elements
O(n) because each push must shift all elements up
O(log n) because the index must be updated in a balanced tree
O(n log n) because resizing happens every operation
drafts.explanation: Each push/pop only increments or decrements the top index, so it runs in constant time; the most plausible distractor assumes shifts are needed, but arrays do not shift when tracking just the top.
drafts.rationale: Simple constant-time stack operations sharply separate beginners from those mixing up array shifts.

drafts.questionText: Why can a circular buffer dequeue without moving the remaining elements?
options: Because head moves forward and wraps modulo buffer length
Because elements are shifted one slot each time
Because the buffer is kept sorted so removal is at a fixed location
Because dequeue simply swaps with the tail entry
drafts.explanation: Advancing the head pointer modulo the buffer size logically removes the element without shifting; the plausible distractor imagines shifting every element, which defeats the circular design.
drafts.rationale: Understanding pointer movement in circular buffers distinguishes students who know the abstraction from those who imagine physical shifts.

drafts.questionText: Why does keeping the load factor below a threshold preserve average O(1) lookup time in a hash table?
options: Because fewer entries per bucket reduce collision chains
Because the bucket array is fully sorted when load is low
Because entries are stored in a binary search tree per bucket
Because resizing stops any future insertions
drafts.explanation: Lower load factor means each bucket has fewer collisions, keeping average probes constant; the nearest distractor wrongly attributes it to sorting, which hash tables do not maintain.
drafts.rationale: Collision density directly drives average-case cost, so this question cleanly differentiates understanding.

drafts.questionText: Which statement best describes why a complete binary tree has height O(log n)?
options: Each level doubles nodes so height grows logarithmically with total nodes
Each node holds pointers to its parent and both children
Only left children are filled first, so height stays constant
The tree stores all nodes in an array for O(1) access
drafts.explanation: Completeness fills levels fully, so node count roughly doubles per level, making height logarithmic; the plausible distractor claiming constant height ignores node growth.
drafts.rationale: Linking level fullness to logarithmic height clearly contrasts students who reason about node counts versus irrelevant properties.

drafts.questionText: Given the stack implementation below, what is the amortized cost of push and pop operations, and why does pop stay O(1)?
options: O(1) amortized for both because Python lists resize geometrically
O(1) push but O(n) pop because removing shifts every element
O(n) amortized push due to copying before each append
O(log n) for both because the underlying array is a tree
drafts.explanation: Appending/popping at the end of a Python list is amortized O(1) since resizing copies only occasionally, unlike the distractor claiming pop shifts all elements which is false because no shifting occurs at the end.
drafts.rationale: The question cleanly distinguishes students who know amortized resizing from those who conflate pop with removing from the front.

drafts.questionText: Using this circular buffer, after enqueuing five values into capacity 5 and then dequeuing three, how many additional enqueues can succeed before OverflowError?
options: 3, because dequeuing frees slots and the count tracks available capacity
2, because wrap-around prevents filling more than two additional slots
5, because the buffer simply reuses all slots after dequeues
0, because once full it never accepts more even after dequeues
drafts.explanation: After removing three items, count drops to two, so three new enqueues can succeed; the distractor claiming wrap-around limits to two confuses physical indices with the count.
drafts.rationale: Moderate discrimination taps whether students track logical count versus raw index behavior.

drafts.questionText: In this chained hash map, which change keeps expected insert time near O(1) as more key/value pairs are added?
options: Resize (e.g., double) the bucket array when size equals its length to bound the load factor
Change bucket lists into linked lists, since array-backed buckets already degrade
Always recompute hash(key) without adjusting bucket count, keeping modulus fixed
Keep inserting and only rebuild buckets once to avoid repeated work
drafts.explanation: Doubling the bucket array when size hits its length keeps the average load per bucket bounded, unlike just switching to linked lists (which already exist) or ignoring load increases, making A correct and B irrelevant.
drafts.rationale: This question separates students who know resizing keeps load factor constant from those who think linked structures alone guarantee O(1).

drafts.questionText: Why does the deque in this sliding-window max code allow emitting each window's maximum in O(1) time per window?
options: The deque stores indices in decreasing order of their values so the front is always the window max
The deque stores only values greater than the window size to limit iteration
Indices are kept in insertion order so every value gets checked twice
Each time we append a value we rotate the deque to keep it balanced
drafts.explanation: Maintaining decreasing-value indices lets the front give the max without scanning windows; the distractor about storing values greater than the window size misreads how monotonic queues work.
drafts.rationale: This probes understanding of the invariant that makes the sliding-window max efficient versus thinking about arbitrary deque tricks.

drafts.questionText: For a sparse directed graph where you repeatedly test whether a particular edge exists and only occasionally iterate the out-neighbors of low-degree vertices, which adjacency representation best balances fast existence checks with Θ(V+E) space?
options: Adjacency matrix implemented with bitsets per row
Adjacency list where each neighbor set is a hash table
Sorted array of edges with binary search for each vertex pair
Edge list augmented by a Boolean presence indicator per vertex
drafts.explanation: Hash-based adjacency lists give expected O(1) edge tests and iterate neighbors in Θ(out-degree) while keeping O(V+E) space; a dense matrix wastes Θ(V²) space, so it’s wrong for sparse graphs.
drafts.rationale: The question sharply distinguishes between sparse vs dense representations, so a moderate discriminator is appropriate.

drafts.questionText: An LRU cache must insert keys, evict the least-recently-used entry, and delete any entry when given its key, all in O(1). Which structure combination supports every operation?
options: Singly linked list for recency plus hash map from key to list node
Doubly linked list for recency plus hash map from key to list node
Circular buffer for recency plus hash map from key to buffer index
Min-heap over access timestamps plus hash map to heap index
drafts.explanation: The doubly linked list keeps recency order with O(1) removals via stored node pointers, unlike the singly-linked or heap-based choices whose removals need prior nodes or O(log n) adjustments.
drafts.rationale: The right answer requires recalling why two-way links are necessary, making it a sharp discriminator.

drafts.questionText: Which data structure lets you maintain a dynamic set of integers and support insert, delete, and find-next-greater (successor) all in O(log n)?
options: Unordered hash set
Max-heap keyed by value
Balanced binary search tree (e.g., red-black tree)
Adjacency list per vertex
drafts.explanation: Balanced BSTs guarantee logarithmic insert/delete and can navigate to a successor using tree structure; hash sets and heaps lack ordered traversal for successor queries.
drafts.rationale: Moderate discrimination fits because some learners might misapply heaps or hashes without ordering.

drafts.questionText: You need a priority queue where merges of two queues happen very often and delete-min stays O(log n). Which heap variant should you choose?
options: Binary heap stored in array
Binomial heap using forest of binomial trees
Fibonacci heap with lazy melding
Pair of stacks emulating priority order
drafts.explanation: Fibonacci heaps support amortized O(1) melds while keeping delete-min O(log n); binary heaps take Θ(n) to merge, and stacks cannot maintain priorities.
drafts.rationale: This question contrasts heap merge trade-offs, so a strong discriminator is justified.

drafts.questionText: During a linear search of an unsorted array, if the target happens to be the very first element, what is the time complexity?
options: O(log n)
O(n)
O(n log n)
drafts.explanation: Finding the first element stops immediately so it takes constant time; O(log n) would imply halving the array, which linear search never does.
drafts.rationale: Simple best-case runtime recognition separates early-stage learners without needing deep reasoning.

drafts.questionText: When the input list is already sorted in ascending order, insertion sort only compares each item with its predecessor. What is its time complexity in that best case?
options: O(n)
O(n log n)
O(log n)
drafts.explanation: Each element is compared once and no shifts are needed, yielding linear time; O(n log n) describes average behavior, not this optimized scenario.
drafts.rationale: The question keys on recognizing best-case behavior, which is clear for learners who know how insertion sort behaves on sorted input.

drafts.questionText: What does it mean for a sorting algorithm to be “in place”?
options: Rearranges the input using only a constant amount of extra memory
Builds a separate array for the result and leaves the input untouched
Uses recursion to break the list down until single elements remain
Runs in O(n) time regardless of the initial order
drafts.explanation: In-place algorithms modify the existing array with minimal extra storage; allocating a new array contradicts that property even if it leaves the input untouched.
drafts.rationale: Distinguishing memory usage requirements is straightforward for early learners, so discrimination remains moderate while keeping difficulty low.

drafts.questionText: Why does a stable sorting algorithm matter when records can share the same key?
options: It keeps equal-key records in the same relative order they had before
It always executes faster than an unstable sort on the same data
It arranges equal keys so the newest records come first
It pairs each key with the number of times it appears
drafts.explanation: Stability preserves the input order of duplicates, which is required when later processing depends on that order; performance claims about faster execution are unrelated to stability.
drafts.rationale: Understanding stability is binary and easy to check conceptually, so discrimination can stay moderate for this introductory question.

drafts.questionText: While implementing a bottom-up DP solution, why must you fill table entries in dependency order?
options: So each state sees its prerequisites already computed before use
To avoid hitting recursion limits from deep stack frames
Because the table needs to remain sorted to index correctly
To ensure a greedy choice remains optimal at every step
drafts.explanation: Filling entries after dependencies guarantees you reuse computed results; worrying about recursion depth (B) is irrelevant once the recursion is eliminated.
drafts.rationale: Medium discrimination since a clear misconception (B) exists but many students grasp dependency order.

drafts.questionText: In the Master theorem recurrence T(n)=aT(n/b)+f(n), when does f(n) dominate and determine the asymptotic complexity?
options: f(n)=Ω(n^{log_b a+ε}) for some ε>0 and f(n/b)≤c·f(n) for c<1
f(n)=Θ(n^{log_b a}) so it matches the equal case
f(n)=O(n^{log_b a-ε}) to shrink faster than recursive work
f(n)=Ω(n^{log_b a+ε}) even without a regularity condition
drafts.explanation: Case 3 requires both a polynomially larger f(n) and the regularity condition; option D omits the regularity check needed to guarantee domination.
drafts.rationale: Needs moderate discrimination because many students know the growth condition but forget regularity, so IRT A is slightly above 1.

drafts.questionText: Why can BFS record a vertex’s shortest distance as soon as it is first dequeued in an unweighted graph?
options: Vertices are visited in order of their distance from the source, so first visit is shortest
Because BFS never enqueues a vertex more than once
Neighbors are processed in sorted order to match distances
BFS marks vertices visited before exploring any edge to them
drafts.explanation: The queue order reflects increasing distance, so the first time you dequeue a vertex is via the fewest edges; simply not enqueuing duplicates (B) doesn’t guarantee the first visit is shortest.
drafts.rationale: Discrimination is modest because the correct logic contrasts with a common but weaker belief about BFS behavior.

drafts.questionText: Memoizing a recursive function gives polynomial time only when the number of distinct subproblem states is polynomial in input size. What keeps that state count polynomial?
options: The recursion arguments are drawn from a set whose size grows polynomially with input size
Each recursive call splits the problem evenly, keeping the tree balanced
Sorting the input beforehand bounds the recursion depth
Greedy choices ensure only constant extra state is introduced
drafts.explanation: Memoization helps when the recursion’s arguments are limited to polynomially many combinations; balancing the recursion tree (B) doesn’t by itself limit distinct argument tuples.
drafts.rationale: Average discrimination since students either grasp what defines distinct states or they don’t, aligning with a moderate IRT A.

drafts.questionText: Given the LIS implementation below, why does its runtime remain O(n²) even when arr is mostly sorted?
options: Because each i still loops over every j<i, so the nested comparisons stay quadratic regardless of order
Because max(dp[i], dp[j] + 1) scans all dp entries and adds an extra n factor
Because dp lacks memoization, so each subproblem is recomputed from scratch
Because sorting arr beforehand incurs its own Θ(n log n) cost that dominates the loop
drafts.explanation: The double loop ensures every pair i>j is examined, so sortedness doesn’t reduce the Θ(n²) comparisons, whereas option B is wrong since max compares only two values, not the whole dp array.
drafts.rationale: The question hinges on understanding loop nesting, which sharply distinguishes students who grasp quadratic interactions (high discrimination) and is moderately hard (irtB near 1).

drafts.questionText: In the weighted interval scheduling helper below, what prevents the recursion from exploring an exponential number of subsets?
options: Memoization records each index i once, so the include/exclude choices are resolved from cache rather than recomputed
Sorting by end time eliminates overlaps so there is no branching in the recursion tree
The while loop jumps directly to the next compatible interval in constant time, fixing the recursion depth
Tail recursion optimization reuses stack frames, preventing exponential call growth
drafts.explanation: Memoization ensures helper(i) runs once per i, turning exponential branching into linear states, whereas option B wrongly assumes sorting removes the binary choice between include and exclude.
drafts.rationale: Reasoning about memoization’s effect on recursion complexity is subtle, making it a sharp discriminator at high difficulty.

drafts.questionText: Why can this quickselect implementation degrade to O(n²) time even though it selects a random pivot?
options: A bad pivot choice repeatedly produces very unbalanced partitions, so each recursion handles almost the entire array
The list comprehensions rebuild new lists each time, so even good pivots keep the runtime quadratic
Equality-heavy pivots block any progress because the algorithm keeps selecting the same bucket
When k moves backward the recursion revisits previously processed segments, doubling work
drafts.explanation: Although pivoting is random, it can repeatedly choose extreme values, producing skewed partitions that lead to Θ(n²) worst-case work; option B misattributes the worst case to list construction, which only adds constant extra work per level.
drafts.rationale: Distinguishing expected versus worst-case behavior requires multi-step reasoning about random pivots, so I rated it as a strong discriminator and high difficulty.

drafts.questionText: Why does the multi-source BFS below correctly record the shortest distance from each node to the closest start?
options: BFS expands nodes level by level from all starts, so the first time a node is visited the distance equals the shortest path
Using adjacency lists means each node appears only once in the queue, preventing redundant visits
Storing distances in a dict preserves the order of starts and thus enforces correct priority
Enqueuing all starts simultaneously forces a deterministic expansion order, stopping longer paths from being considered first
drafts.explanation: Because BFS processes nodes in order of increasing hop count from the set of starts, the first visit is the minimum distance, whereas option D wrongly attributes correctness to queue ordering rather than level-wise expansion.
drafts.rationale: Evaluating why multi-source BFS preserves shortest paths needs solid grasp of traversal order, so it sharply separates upper-level understanding at hard difficulty.

drafts.questionText: When should you prefer composition over inheritance in an object-oriented design?
options: When you need to reuse behavior without demanding an "is-a" relationship
When you want to share code and guarantee the derived type matches the base type
When the shared functionality must be enforced by a common interface
When subclassing would reduce the number of classes you write
drafts.explanation: Composition is correct because it allows reusing behavior without implying an "is-a" relationship, whereas insisting on shared code via subclassing (option B) still forces that relationship even when it might be inappropriate.
drafts.rationale: This question cleanly separates students who understand when a relationship is required (composition) from those who default to inheritance, so a moderate discriminator is appropriate.

drafts.questionText: Why do we often wrap private fields with getter/setter methods in OOP languages?
options: To control how the field is read or updated and preserve invariants
Because the compiler forbids accessing private fields directly even inside the same class
To avoid documenting the field, since getters/setters automatically do that
Because public fields cannot be serialized without accessors
drafts.explanation: Getters/setters allow the class to validate or transform values, while compiler restrictions (option B) are false—private fields are accessible within their own class.
drafts.rationale: Students who grasp encapsulation will quickly see that control over access is the key, so the discrimination is somewhat gentle.

drafts.questionText: Which SOLID principle states a class should have only one reason to change?
options: Single Responsibility Principle
Open/Closed Principle
Dependency Inversion Principle
Interface Segregation Principle
drafts.explanation: The Single Responsibility Principle is about limiting reasons for change, while the Open/Closed Principle (option B) is about being open to extension but closed to modification, a different focus.
drafts.rationale: This question directly tests recognition of a well-known principle, providing a clear signal for discriminating beginners.

drafts.questionText: Which statement best describes abstraction in object-oriented programming?
options: Exposing only the necessary operations while hiding implementation details
Guaranteeing that every class must inherit from a base implementation
Restricting access to data members using language access modifiers
Making every method virtual so derived types can override behaviour
drafts.explanation: Abstraction focuses on hiding implementation details and exposing only needed operations, while option B incorrectly equates abstraction with mandatory inheritance.
drafts.rationale: Testing the core definition of abstraction yields strong discrimination for students who understand the concept versus those equating it with other OOP features.

drafts.questionText: Given the snippet, what principle justifies why the `BankAccount` constructor uses `virtual` `Initialize` even though derived classes override it?
options: Using a virtual initializer lets derived types customize their state before use
It avoids constructor chaining so derived constructors stay simpler
Virtual calls prevent derived fields from being null during base setup
The base constructor only runs after all overrides execute automatically
drafts.explanation: By calling a virtual setup method the base constructor delegates part of its initialization to derived overrides; the most plausible distractor (C) is wrong because virtual calls from the base still run before derived fields are initialized.
drafts.rationale: Medium question about virtual setup explains extra discrimination but not extreme; difficulty aligns with irtB≈0.2.

drafts.questionText: Why does the output always show the `Dog` sound when iterating through the `animals` array?
options: The overridden method on Dog is invoked thanks to dynamic dispatch via virtual methods
Arrays always preserve the runtime type so overridden members behave like static ones
Animal.Speak is abstract so the base implementation still runs after the override
Each element is boxed so the overridden method is the only available version
drafts.explanation: The runtime dispatch mechanism calls Dog.Speak even when referenced as Animal; option B sounds plausible but confuses runtime dispatch with storage details.
drafts.rationale: Moderate discrimination focused on understanding polymorphism, so irtA just below 1.0 and irtB near zero fits.

drafts.questionText: What does the `Printer` interface guarantee when `ClientPrint` accepts it?
options: ClientPrint can work with any implementation that adheres to the Printer contract
Printer enforces all clients must inherit from ConsolePrinter
Interface usage forces Print to be static so no instance state matters
The Print method runs only once because interfaces cache results
drafts.explanation: Interfaces allow any conforming implementation to be passed, while the most plausible distractor (B) is wrong because interfaces do not mandate inheritance from a specific class.
drafts.rationale: Conceptual question about interface contracts with moderate discrimination; irtB slightly negative since medium level.

drafts.questionText: Why does `DerivedProcessor.Process()` get invoked even though `processor` is typed as `BaseProcessor`?
options: Virtual method dispatch uses the runtime type of the object, not the reference type
DerivedProcessor hides Process so only its version is visible even via base typing
Base references always call the base implementation unless sealed overrides exist
Process executes twice: once for base and once for derived automatically
drafts.explanation: Virtual dispatch picks the derived override at runtime; the most plausible distractor (B) misunderstands overriding versus hiding.
drafts.rationale: Conceptual OOP polymorphism question with average discrimination and slightly positive difficulty.

drafts.questionText: A base class defines `virtual bool Start(int intensity)` that accepts 0–5. A derived class overrides it and immediately returns false when `intensity == 0`. Why might client code holding a base reference fail when it gets a derived instance?
options: Strengthening the precondition so callers that relied on intensity 0 now break
Weakening the precondition so the derived class is too permissive
Changing the return type makes the override incompatible
Failing to seal the override allows further overrides that violate encapsulation
drafts.explanation: The derived override forbids a value the base allowed, so substituting it violates Liskov; weakening the precondition (option B) would still let the derived accept every base value and stay safe.
drafts.rationale: Detecting Liskov violations requires sensitive discrimination, so I set a moderately high irtA and a difficulty-aligned irtB above 1.

drafts.questionText: A `ReportGenerator` class switches on a `ReportFormat` enum to build each report, so every new format forces editing the switch. Which SOLID principle is being violated by that design?
options: Open/Closed principle, because the class must change for every new format
Single Responsibility principle, because report generation and formatting share a class
Dependency Inversion principle, because the class depends on a concrete enum
Interface Segregation principle, because the enum exposes unused format choices
drafts.explanation: Open/Closed is broken because adding formats mutates existing code; blaming Single Responsibility (option B) misidentifies the symptom, since the problem is about extension without modification.
drafts.rationale: The question sharply distinguishes students who grasp open/closed, so I chose a solid discrimination and difficulty near the upper range.

drafts.questionText: If `IAnimalFactory.Create()` returns `Animal`, why is it valid for `DogFactory` to override it and return `Dog`, while callers treat the result as `Animal`?
options: Covariant return types let an override return a subtype while matching the base signature
Because every use site downcasts the result to `Dog` before calling any methods
Because interfaces in the host language ignore return types when checking overrides
Because `DogFactory` really defines a new method that hides the base method
drafts.explanation: Most modern type systems allow covariant returns so the override can return `Dog`; suggesting every caller downcasts (option B) misreads the compile-time contract, which already guarantees safety.
drafts.rationale: Understanding covariant returns demands precise reasoning, so I chose a strong discrimination and set the difficulty near the upper bound.

drafts.questionText: A third-party sealed `Logger` provides a non-virtual `Log()` you cannot override. How can you add metrics while keeping the existing client code untouched?
options: Wrap the Logger in a new class implementing the same interface, delegate to it, and add metric logic
Subclass the Logger and override `Log()` to record metrics before calling base
Add an extension method that records metrics and then calls the sealed `Log()`
Edit every call site to record metrics and then call the original `Logger.Log()`
drafts.explanation: Composition lets you add metrics without needing inheritance; subclassing a sealed type (option B) is impossible, so it cannot be the correct strategy.
drafts.rationale: Recognizing when composition must replace inheritance is a crisp judgment, so I assigned a solid discrimination and a difficulty above 1.

drafts.questionText: If a column is declared UNIQUE but not a primary key, what must hold true?
options: Non-null values cannot repeat, but NULL may appear multiple times
It behaves identically to a primary key in every way
Every row must provide a value, so NULL is forbidden
The server automatically creates a clustered index for it
drafts.explanation: A UNIQUE constraint allows NULLs to repeat while still requiring each actual value to be distinct; unlike option B, it does not forbid NULLs and does not automatically become the identifier for the row.
drafts.rationale: Straightforward reasoning about constraints keeps discrimination standard and difficulty low.

drafts.questionText: Which clause should you add to SELECT to remove duplicate rows from the result?
options: DISTINCT
GROUP BY
HAVING
UNIQUE
drafts.explanation: DISTINCT explicitly removes duplicate rows, whereas GROUP BY merely organizes rows into groups and HAVING filters those groups; UNIQUE is not a valid SELECT clause.
drafts.rationale: A basic SQL knowledge check with a clear correct clause and plausible near-miss distractors.

drafts.questionText: Why must every non-aggregated column in SELECT appear in GROUP BY when using aggregates?
options: To define how those columns determine each output group
To ensure results are automatically sorted
To run the query before aggregation begins
To use fewer disk pages while scanning
drafts.explanation: SQL mandates grouping of all non-aggregated columns so the query knows which rows form each output row; grouping does not control sorting (option B) nor query timing.
drafts.rationale: Requires understanding GROUP BY rules, so default discrimination and an easy difficulty setting suffice.

drafts.questionText: Which ACID property ensures a committed transaction’s changes survive a system crash?
options: Durability
Atomicity
Isolation
Consistency
drafts.explanation: Durability guarantees persistence across crashes; Atomicity only ensures all-or-nothing execution, so it does not promise survival once committed.
drafts.rationale: Classic ACID recall question with clear right answer and plausible distractors from the other properties.

drafts.questionText: A query joins employees to departments, filters on department_id, and orders the result by salary. Which single index best reduces page reads for both filtering and ordering?
options: A composite index on (department_id, salary) covering employees
A single-column index on salary
A single-column index on department_id
A composite index on (salary, department_id)
drafts.explanation: Indexing (department_id, salary) lets the planner narrow rows by department and produce sorted salaries without extra work; indexing only salary still scans all departments, so it leaves the filter expensive.
drafts.rationale: The question distinguishes between similar indexes, so it should discriminate moderately (1.4) and be average in difficulty (0.1).

drafts.questionText: Enrollment(student_id PK, course_id PK, grade, student_major) repeats each major per enrollment. To reach 3NF while keeping grade per course, which schema change is best?
options: Move student_major to a Students table keyed by student_id and reference it via FK from Enrollment
Add a UNIQUE constraint on student_major to avoid duplicates
Make (student_id, student_major) the new primary key for Enrollment
Keep student_major but only write it when grade is NULL
drafts.explanation: Separating student_major into Students removes the dependency on the composite key while keeping enrollment grades; UNIQUE or new PK do not remove the partial dependency and still repeat majors.
drafts.rationale: Medium schema reasoning with a clear correct improvement gives moderate discrimination and neutral difficulty.

drafts.questionText: When aggregating rows with COUNT(*) per category, why must a filter on the aggregated count appear after GROUP BY?
options: WHERE filters rows before aggregation, so it cannot refer to the aggregate value
WHERE can filter aggregates but only in subqueries
HAVING is shorthand for WHERE when aggregates are involved
GROUP BY runs after HAVING, so HAVING sees raw rows
drafts.explanation: WHERE runs before the grouping stage, so aggregate expressions aren’t yet computed; HAVING runs afterward and can filter the computed count, so claiming WHERE can do it is wrong.
drafts.rationale: Understanding query evaluation order separates learners, so a standard discrimination (1.0) and slight positive difficulty fit.

drafts.questionText: To ensure deleting a customer automatically removes their orders while preventing orphan rows, which foreign-key action should be declared on Orders(customer_id)?
options: ON DELETE CASCADE
ON DELETE SET NULL
ON DELETE RESTRICT
ON DELETE NO ACTION
drafts.explanation: CASCADE ensures child rows go away with the parent, preventing orphans; RESTRICT or NO ACTION block the delete but leave rows intact, so they do not remove orders automatically.
drafts.rationale: Differentiating referential actions is moderately discriminating and slightly above average in difficulty due to nuance.

drafts.questionText: Shipments records store the customer country and its delivery zone, even though each country always maps to a single zone. Which schema change best eliminates the transitive dependency while still letting you report a shipment’s zone?
options: Create CountryZones(country VARCHAR PRIMARY KEY, shipping_zone VARCHAR), replace country in Shipments with FK and join when you need the zone
Add UNIQUE(customer_country, shipping_zone) to Shipments and keep both columns there
Add shipping_zone to Customers and populate it from Shipments when inserting
Make shipping_zone a generated column derived from customer_country within Shipments
drafts.explanation: Moving the zone lookup to a separate table removes the transitive dependency and keeps Shipments in 3NF; a UNIQUE constraint or computed column still stores the redundant zone per shipment, and pushing the zone into Customers keeps the redundancy tied to shipments.
drafts.rationale: The question sharply differentiates students who grasp functional dependencies versus those reasoning superficially about constraints, so a higher discrimination is appropriate.

drafts.questionText: To minimize random I/O for this query, which index on Orders best supports the WHERE filter, the ORDER BY, and the join to Customers?
drafts.codeSnippet:
SELECT o.order_id, c.name
FROM Orders o
JOIN Customers c ON c.customer_id = o.customer_id
WHERE o.status = 'SHIPPED'
ORDER BY o.order_date DESC;
options: CREATE INDEX ON Orders(status, order_date DESC, customer_id);
CREATE INDEX ON Orders(order_date DESC);
CREATE INDEX ON Orders(customer_id, status, order_date);
CREATE INDEX ON Orders(status, customer_id);
drafts.explanation: An index starting with status lets the planner satisfy the WHERE, continuing with order_date DESC maintains the sort order, and trailing customer_id supports the join; the other indexes either miss the sort order or the filtering column order needed for this multi-step plan.
drafts.rationale: High discrimination is warranted because understanding why index column order matters separates stronger learners from the rest.

drafts.questionText: Under REPEATABLE READ, what can Transaction T1 still observe after Transaction T2 inserts a qualifying row and commits between two identical reads?
options: T1 will never see the row inserted by T2 because REPEATABLE READ reuses the original snapshot
T1 will see the new row because REPEATABLE READ allows phantom reads from concurrent inserts
T1 may see the row only if T2 committed before T1’s first SELECT
T1 will see the row if it re-runs the query inside a fresh transaction
drafts.explanation: REPEATABLE READ guarantees that repeated queries within the same transaction observe the same snapshot, so T1 can’t see T2’s new row; the plausible distractor about seeing it if T2 committed earlier confuses timing with isolation guarantees.
drafts.rationale: Moderate discrimination because the key idea is understanding snapshot consistency, which some students grasp and others don’t.

drafts.questionText: Which index lets the planner filter by date, avoid sorting, and compute regional revenue without scanning the entire Sales table?
drafts.codeSnippet:
SELECT region, SUM(total) AS revenue
FROM Sales
WHERE order_date >= '2024-01-01'
GROUP BY region
HAVING SUM(total) > 100000;
options: CREATE INDEX ON Sales(order_date, region, total);
CREATE INDEX ON Sales(total, region);
CREATE INDEX ON Sales(region, order_date);
CREATE INDEX ON Sales(order_date, customer_id)
drafts.explanation: Ordering by order_date first lets the filter be index-supported, including region allows the GROUP BY to use the index order, and total makes the index covering for the aggregation; other indexes either omit order_date first or lack total, blocking early pruning.
drafts.rationale: Discrimination is elevated because combining filtering, grouping, and covering behavior requires nuanced reasoning.

drafts.questionText: What does HTTPS primarily protect when submitting a login form compared to plain HTTP?
options: It stops attackers from reading credentials as they travel across the network
It ensures the server checks the user’s password instead of the client
It prevents SQL injection in the login handler
It forces browsers to reject unauthorized cookies
drafts.explanation: HTTPS encrypts the traffic so eavesdroppers cannot read credentials; the most plausible distractor claims it prevents SQL injection, which is unrelated to transport encryption.
drafts.rationale: This question cleanly distinguishes basic transport security, so IRT discrimination stays near default and difficulty sits on the easier end.

drafts.questionText: Why should a session cookie include the HttpOnly flag before sending to the browser?
options: To block JavaScript from reading the cookie and mitigate token theft via XSS
To make sure the browser only sends it over HTTPS
To require the server to revalidate the cookie on every request
To keep the cookie from being stored in the browser cache
drafts.explanation: HttpOnly prevents scripts from accessing the cookie, lowering XSS risk; the closest distractor says it ensures HTTPS-only transmission, but that is the Secure flag’s job.
drafts.rationale: HttpOnly’s benefit is widely taught, so moderate discrimination suffices and difficulty is low.

drafts.questionText: What is the main security reason a password-reset link should expire shortly after it is issued?
options: To shrink the time window an attacker can reuse a leaked link
To reduce the number of rows in the reset_token table
To force users to request a new link every day
To notify the original user that the request has completed
drafts.explanation: A short expiry limits how long a stolen reset link remains valid; the plausible distractor about database cleanup is operational but not the primary security rationale.
drafts.rationale: The security benefit is straightforward, so lower discrimination and an easy difficulty rating are appropriate.

drafts.questionText: What does rate limiting failed login attempts help prevent?
options: Automated brute-force guessing of a user’s password
Users from reusing the same password on multiple accounts
Session fixation by allowing multiple concurrent sessions
Cross-site request forgery during authentication
drafts.explanation: Rate limiting slows down brute-force guessing; the plausible distractor about preventing users reusing passwords addresses policy, not brute-force control.
drafts.rationale: The concept is basic but differentiates understanding of brute-force mitigation, so a standard discrimination and low difficulty work.

drafts.questionText: The snippet below shows how user comments are rendered. What change best prevents stored XSS?
options: Use textContent rather than innerHTML when injecting the comment
Limit comments to 200 characters before rendering them
Extend the regex to remove more tag names before rendering
Replace each '<' and '>' with their HTML entities before rendering
drafts.explanation: textContent treats the string as literal text, stopping any markup from executing, whereas expanding the regex is brittle and still lets other tags slip through.
drafts.rationale: The question sharply contrasts two rendering techniques, so a moderate discrimination is appropriate and the difficulty suits an average mid-level student.

drafts.questionText: After login, this route redirects the client to the path in req.query.next. What change best prevents an open redirect?
options: Allow redirects only when req.query.next matches an approved list of internal paths
Always append req.query.next to the root so users see their intended destination
Reject login attempts when req.query.next is present to avoid uncertainty
Store req.query.next in a cookie and redirect based on the cookie after authentication
drafts.explanation: Validating against an allowlist lets the app respect intended navigation while preventing attackers from forcing arbitrary external URLs, unlike simply appending the parameter or rejecting requests which either remain unsafe or hurt UX.
drafts.rationale: This question contrasts secure vs insecure redirect handling, so a standard discrimination and neutral difficulty rating match the concept.

drafts.questionText: The worker handles actions sent over postMessage. What change best prevents remote code execution?
options: Map allowed action names to handler functions and invoke the matching one without eval
Escape punctuation in message.action before passing it to eval so it cannot close strings
Only run eval when message.origin matches a trusted source
Wrap the eval call in try/catch so errors stop further execution
drafts.explanation: Mapping names to functions eliminates the need for dynamic evaluation, whereas escaping punctuation or catching errors still executes arbitrary code and therefore leaves the RCE bug.
drafts.rationale: The question contrasts secure dispatching with evident insecure eval usage, so a slightly higher discrimination fits.

drafts.questionText: This helper encrypts data with AES-256-CBC. What makes the ciphertext unsafe even though the key is secret?
options: Reusing the all-zero IV means encrypting the same message twice leaks their XOR stream
AES-256-CBC itself lacks integrity so the ciphertext can always be tampered with
Not appending an HMAC means modifications are silently accepted
Using a fixed IV forces the key to be weaker than 256 bits
drafts.explanation: A constant IV causes repeated keystreams so patterns between ciphertexts leak, whereas issues like missing HMAC are real but unrelated to the fixed IV vulnerability highlighted here.
drafts.rationale: The question zeroes in on IV reuse versus other ciphertext properties, so a standard discrimination and slightly above-average difficulty capture that focus.

drafts.questionText: A microservice validates JWTs issued by a shared identity provider by checking the signature and expiration, but it ignores the `aud` claim. What is the primary risk?
options: Accepting tokens minted for a different API and granting them access to this service
Treating every token as scoped to the caller’s identity even without checking `sub`
Allowing tokens to be reused after rotation because `aud` is missing
Failing to detect when the token issuer changes over time
drafts.explanation: If an attacker reuses a token whose `aud` names another API but the same issuer, the service still trusts it; checking `aud` ensures the token was meant for this service, while simply relying on `sub` or rotation timing doesn’t prove audience.
drafts.rationale: High discriminator because only students who understand audience restriction versus basic token fields can spot why signature+expiry alone fail.

drafts.questionText: A JWT consumer supports both RS256 and HS256 by reading the `alg` header and verifying accordingly. Why does this make the system vulnerable?
options: An attacker can switch the header to HS256 and reuse the provider’s public key as the HMAC secret to forge tokens
It allows the provider to rotate keys without updating the consumer, so old tokens remain valid
The consumer will accept tokens signed by any key listed in a `kid` header, bypassing issuer checks
If the token issuer changes algorithms, the consumer might reject genuine tokens
drafts.explanation: Trusting the `alg` header lets an attacker supply HS256 and exploit the public RSA key as a shared HMAC secret, while the other options either describe key rotation logistics or issuer failures that don’t arise from trusting `alg`.
drafts.rationale: High discrimination because only learners aware of JWT algorithm confusion attacks will choose the right trade-off.

drafts.questionText: An API authenticates requests by verifying an HMAC over the request body plus a client-supplied timestamp, allowing ±5 minutes. Why is this still susceptible to replay attacks?
options: Because the same request can be resent within the window without any nonce or counter to make it unique
Because timestamps are predictable and let attackers forge future HMACs
Because TLS already protects integrity, so adding HMAC adds no benefit
Because the server should also encrypt the body before HMACing to prevent replays
drafts.explanation: Replays succeed while the timestamp is fresh, so the missing uniqueness (nonce or server-tracked counter) is the gap; timestamps being predictable or encryption are unrelated to replay protection.
drafts.rationale: Moderately sharp discriminator since only students who grasp nonce vs timestamp trade-offs avoid the tempting timestamp prediction trap.

drafts.questionText: A login endpoint rate-limits by client IP (five attempts per minute). Attackers now use hundreds of IPs to try every password for one account. What adjustment best closes this gap without causing widespread lockouts?
options: Also rate-limit by account identifier so repeated failures on the same username trigger throttling
Require CAPTCHA for every attempt so bots can't perform distributed guessing
Increase the per-IP limit to tolerate more users sharing NATed addresses
Log every failure and alert admins if any account hits the IP limit
drafts.explanation: Per-account throttling blocks distributed guessing on the same username even when IPs vary, while CAPTCHA or alerts are secondary defenses and raising the IP limit worsens the problem.
drafts.rationale: Reasonably discriminating because the right adjustment balances distributed attack mitigation without inducing denials of service, unlike other plausible mitigations.
